summary:
The Tripitaka Koreana (Goryeo Tripitaka) is the world's most complete and accurate extant collection of Buddhist scriptures, comprising 81,258 woodblocks carved during the Goryeo Dynasty of Korea between 1237 and 1248 AD — a second carving undertaken after the first set (completed 1087, destroyed by the [[event:2227|Mongol invasions]]) was burned. The name 'Tripitaka' (Sanskrit for 'three baskets') refers to the three canonical sections of Buddhist scripture; the Korean collection contains approximately 52 million Chinese characters engraved onto wooden printing blocks with extraordinary precision and consistency, compiled by Korean monks who collated multiple Chinese, Khitan, and Song dynasty versions to produce the most accurate text. The project was initiated as a prayer for divine intervention against the Mongol invasion — a spiritual act of collective supplication by the Goryeo court and clergy.

The physical and scholarly achievement of the Tripitaka Koreana is staggering. Each of the 81,258 woodblocks (each roughly 70cm × 24cm × 2.6–4cm) was prepared from seasoned birch wood, treated with salt water to prevent warping and insect damage, then coated with lacquer after carving. The text was proofread multiple times; errors are extraordinarily rare. The blocks have survived nearly 800 years in the Haeinsa Temple storehouses — a UNESCO World Heritage site — preserved by the temple's exceptional microclimate and ventilation architecture. The Tripitaka Koreana represents not only a religious monument but one of the greatest feats of medieval scholarship and craftsmanship in Asian history: a systematic effort to preserve and transmit a complete intellectual and spiritual tradition in the face of conquest and destruction.
